Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd have these bursts where I'd study for hours straight, then nothing for days. Being a kinesthetic learner, I couldn't just sit and read—I needed to move around, act things out, or write stuff by hand repeatedly. Most of my studying happened late at night when my anxiety was keeping me up anyway, so it wasn't exactly planned or organized.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely driven by deadlines and whatever crisis was happening that week. I'd go days without touching anything academic, then suddenly realize something was due and pull an all-nighter. With 14 online courses to juggle and limited resources at home, I couldn't really stick to a consistent schedule even when I tried.
Q3: Usually panic, to be honest. I'd log in when I suddenly remembered an assignment or when I got an email notification about a deadline. Sometimes boredom would push me to check in, just scrolling through without really doing much. The anxiety of not knowing what I was missing would eventually force me to open it up.
Q4: Not really, no. I tried to make it routine a few times, but something always disrupted it—my living situation isn't the most stable, and resources are tight, so I can't always access things when I want. The closest thing to routine was checking in during my CBT sessions when my therapist would help me structure my week, but that didn't last consistently.
Q5: The videos were actually really helpful because I could watch them while doing other things, like pacing around my room or stretching. I watched over 260 minutes of video content because it felt more engaging than just reading. The discussion posts were also useful because interacting with peers made the material feel more real and less like I was just staring at a screen alone.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and PDFs. They just felt so passive and boring—I'd open them, skim a bit, then zone out completely. Anything that required me to sit still and concentrate for long periods got pushed aside because my anxiety made it impossible to focus that way. I knew they were important but I kept telling myself I'd get to them later.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could actually do something with it, not just absorb information passively. If I could discuss it with someone, apply it somehow, or if it was directly tied to an assignment I needed to pass, then I'd engage. Otherwise, it felt like busy work that just added to my stress without any payoff.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. Like, I knew they could help, but with everything else I was barely managing to complete, adding optional stuff felt impossible. Required activities at least had the pressure of grades behind them, which gave me the adrenaline push I needed. Optional things just made me feel guilty for not doing them.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download materials and study from my phone on the bus or while walking around. I took a lot of handwritten notes because writing things out multiple times helped me remember better than just reading. I also used free YouTube videos and other websites when the course materials weren't clicking for me.
Q10: A lot of my actual learning happened offline. I'd print things out at the library when I could afford it and study from paper, highlighting and annotating. Or I'd record myself explaining concepts and listen back while doing chores. None of that showed up anywhere in the system, but it was real studying.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then work with it for days without going back to the LMS. My internet isn't always reliable at home, so I learned to grab what I needed and work offline. The LMS probably thought I'd abandoned the material when really I was still using it.
Q12: There were several points where everything just felt impossible. My anxiety would spike, and I'd freeze up completely—couldn't even look at the course without feeling overwhelmed. Financial stress at home made it hard to concentrate, and missing classes made me feel so behind that catching up seemed pointless. Those weeks my attendance and engagement basically disappeared.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was exhausted from working or dealing with stuff at home, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to open. The cognitive load of managing 14 courses meant I was constantly triaging—figuring out which fires to put out first. Confusion about assignments made me avoid them entirely sometimes because asking for help felt embarrassing.
Q14: So many times. I'd log in, open videos or readings, and just stare at them while my mind was somewhere else entirely. I was physically present on the platform but mentally checked out, too anxious or bored to actually process anything. Those sessions felt like going through the motions without actually learning.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially the bigger assignments. The anxiety would build up so much that starting felt impossible—I'd tell myself I needed to be in the right headspace first. Important stuff got pushed back because my brain convinced me I couldn't handle it yet, even though waiting only made things worse.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd ignore everything until the deadline was breathing down my neck, then scramble to get something submitted. The LMS became most active for me in the 24-48 hours before due dates. It's not healthy, but that pressure was the only thing that could cut through the boredom and anxiety paralysis.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did affect me, but I didn't always check it right away because I was scared of what it might say. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost and made me want to engage more. Negative or critical feedback sometimes sent me into a spiral where I'd avoid the course for days.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming with so many courses running simultaneously. When modules were clearly laid out with specific deadlines, I could at least panic-plan around them. But when things were vague or self-paced, I'd let them slide indefinitely. The lack of in-person structure made it harder for someone like me who learns by doing.
Q19: Having videos helped because I could engage more actively with them. But the heavy reliance on reading and passive content made it harder for a kinesthetic learner like me. The discussion forums were good because they gave me something to actually do. What made it harder was when everything felt disconnected and I couldn't see how pieces fit together.
Q20: That's kind of me, honestly. I'd spend time in the LMS watching videos, reading discussions, trying to understand things, but then couldn't bring myself to actually complete and submit work. The anxiety of being judged on assignments was different from just browsing content. Sometimes you're trying to learn but the final step of producing something feels impossible.
Q21: Yeah, this also sounds like me in some ways. I did a lot of learning outside the LMS that helped me pass my exams even though my attendance and completion rates were terrible. The system doesn't see you studying from printed notes or practicing concepts while walking around. You can absorb material without constantly being logged in.
Q22: Course logs can show when you're online and what you click on, but they can't measure understanding or real engagement. They miss all the offline studying, the mental processing that happens away from the screen, and the emotional barriers that affect participation. A log might show I watched a video but not that I was dissociating through half of it.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and connecting with the material—feeling curious, wanting to understand, and being mentally present. It's not just logging in or completing tasks mechanically. For me, real engagement happens when I can interact with content physically or socially, when it doesn't feel like I'm just surviving but actually learning.
Q24: The LMS can't show how hard I was fighting just to stay enrolled, or the mental health battles behind every missed deadline. It doesn't capture the learning that happened during my CBT sessions when I'd process course stress, or the studying I did while pacing my room at 2 AM. It also misses that despite everything—the anxiety, the low resources, the chaos—I actually passed, which took more effort than my data suggests.
